npcs.name: The LORD
npcs.role: Creator and Covenant-Maker
npcs.description: YHWH acts directly in Genesis: creating, judging, promising, relenting. He makes covenants he then tests. He destroys the world and regrets it. He chooses [[Jacob]] over [[Esau]] before either is born. He wrestles with [[Jacob]] in the dark. He is not distant or symbolic — he is present and surprising.

log.note: Consequence of Abraham (ally): The binding of Isaac on Moriah — God provides; the covenant confirmed.
Consequence of Jacob (ally): Jacob steals Esau's blessing — Esau weeps, vows to kill him; Jacob must flee.
Consequence of Joseph (ally): Joseph sold into Egypt by his own brothers.
Consequence of Joseph (ally): Joseph reveals himself — forgiveness, reunion, the dream fulfilled.

npcs.name: Adam
npcs.role: The First Man, keeper of the garden
npcs.description: Made from dust, given breath, placed in [[The Garden of Eden|a garden]] with one prohibition. He names every creature. He receives the woman and joins her in the one thing they were told not to do. He blames her when caught. He is every man.

log.note: [[Adam]] and [[Eve]] eat the fruit of the tree of knowledge of good and evil; they hide from God among the trees of the garden.

npcs.name: Eve
npcs.role: The First Woman, mother of all living
npcs.description: She hears the serpent's argument and finds it compelling — the fruit will make them wise, like God. She eats. She gives to [[Adam]]. She names herself Eve, 'mother of all living,' after the curse. The naming is an act of defiance and hope in the same breath.

npcs.name: Cain
npcs.role: The First Murderer, keeper of the earth
npcs.description: He offers the fruits of the ground; [[Abel]] offers fat portions of his flock. God regards [[Abel]]'s offering and not his. The text never explains why. Cain's rage at the injustice is understandable. What he does with it is not. He is marked but not destroyed — God protects the first murderer.

log.note: [[Cain]] kills his brother [[Abel]] in the field; when God asks where [[Abel]] is, [[Cain]] says: am I my brother's keeper?

npcs.name: Abel
npcs.role: Shepherd and first martyr
npcs.description: He exists in the story for three verses and then he is dead. His blood cries from the ground. He has no voice in the text but God hears him. He is the first of many younger brothers the story loves better.

npcs.name: Noah
npcs.role: Righteous survivor, builder of the ark
npcs.description: God finds one righteous man in a generation given to violence, and tells him to build a boat. Noah does exactly what he is told, nothing more. After the flood he plants a vineyard, drinks too much, and curses his son Ham. The first act after survival is often embarrassing.

log.note: God tells [[Noah]] to build an ark; [[Noah]] does exactly what God commands without a word of protest or a question.
[[Noah]] plants a vineyard, drinks too much, and lies uncovered in his tent; Ham sees him and tells his brothers. Shem and Japheth walk in backward with a garment and cover him.

npcs.name: Abraham
npcs.role: Father of Nations, friend of God
npcs.description: He leaves everything at seventy-five on the strength of a promise. He passes his wife off as his sister twice to save his own skin. He laughs when God promises a son at ninety-nine. He rises at dawn to take [[Isaac]] to [[Mount Moriah|Moriah]] without a word to [[Sarah]]. He is the central figure of Genesis: brave, flawed, chosen.

log.note: God tells [[Abraham|Abram]] to leave his country, his kindred, and his father's house for a land he will be shown. [[Abraham|Abram]] leaves. He is seventy-five years old.

Entering Egypt during famine, [[Abraham|Abram]] tells [[Sarah|Sarai]] to say she is his sister to protect himself from men who would kill him for her. She is taken into [[Pharaoh]]'s house. God strikes the household with plagues.
God appears to [[Abraham]] at age ninety-nine and promises a son from [[Sarah]]; [[Abraham]] falls on his face and laughs. He says: shall [[Sarah]], at ninety, bear a child?

[[Abraham]] rises early in the morning and takes [[Isaac]] to [[Mount Moriah]]. He builds the altar, binds his son, and raises the knife. God stops him: now I know you fear God, for you have not withheld your son.

npcs.name: Sarah
npcs.role: Matriarch, mother of the promise
npcs.description: She laughs at the angel's announcement — a ninety-year-old woman bearing a son. She laughed, God notes. She says she didn't laugh. God says she did. The argument is never resolved. She named her son [[Isaac]]: laughter. She is fiercer and harder than [[Abraham]] in protecting her line.

log.note: [[Sarah]] listens from the tent entrance when the angels announce she will bear a son in a year. She laughs to herself. God asks: is anything too hard for the LORD?
Consequence of Abraham (ally): The binding of Isaac on Moriah — God provides; the covenant confirmed.

npcs.name: Hagar
npcs.role: Egyptian servant, mother of Ishmael
npcs.description: [[Sarah]] gives her to [[Abraham]] as a surrogate and then treats her with cruelty when Hagar grows proud of her pregnancy. She flees into the desert. An angel finds her at a spring. God sees her — the text emphasizes this: she is the only person in the Bible to name God. She calls him El-roi: the God who sees me.

log.note: [[Hagar]] flees into the desert after [[Sarah]] mistreats her; the angel of the LORD finds her at a spring on the way to Shur and tells her to return. She names God El-roi: the one who sees me.

npcs.name: Ishmael
npcs.role: Son of Abraham and Hagar, father of twelve princes
npcs.description: The first son, the not-chosen one. He and his mother are sent into the desert with bread and a skin of water when [[Isaac]] is weaned. The water runs out. [[Hagar]] sets the boy under a bush and walks away so she won't watch him die. God hears the boy crying. He will be a great nation too — just not this story's nation.

log.note: Consequence of Abraham (ally): The binding of Isaac on Moriah — God provides; the covenant confirmed.

npcs.name: Melchizedek
npcs.role: Priest-King of Salem, man of mystery
npcs.description: He appears from nowhere to bless [[Abraham]] after battle, brings bread and wine, and disappears. He is priest of El-Elyon — God Most High — before [[Abraham]] ever hears that name. He has no genealogy in the text, no beginning or end. He becomes a type: priesthood outside the bloodline.

log.note: After [[Abraham]]'s victory over the kings, [[Melchizedek]] king of Salem brings bread and wine and blesses him: blessed be [[Abraham|Abram]] by God Most High, creator of heaven and earth.
Consequence of Abraham (ally): The binding of Isaac on Moriah — God provides; the covenant confirmed.

npcs.name: Lot
npcs.role: Abraham's nephew, survivor of Sodom
npcs.description: He chooses the well-watered plain of Jordan and pitches his tent near Sodom. When the city is destroyed he escapes with his daughters. His wife looks back and becomes a pillar of salt. His daughters get him drunk on successive nights and conceive Moab and Ammon. Lot is the character to whom things happen.

log.note: Consequence of Abraham (ally): The binding of Isaac on Moriah — God provides; the covenant confirmed.

npcs.name: Isaac
npcs.role: Son of Promise, quiet patriarch
npcs.description: He is bound on the altar at [[Mount Moriah|Moriah]] and unmade. He is the passive center of his own story: the sacrifice that doesn't happen. He grows up, marries [[Rebekah]], fathers twins he cannot see clearly — literally or otherwise. He blesses [[Jacob]] thinking he is [[Esau]]. He is deceived by his own family.

log.note: Consequence of Abraham (ally): The binding of Isaac on Moriah — God provides; the covenant confirmed.

npcs.name: Rebekah
npcs.role: Matriarch, planner of the deception
npcs.description: She waters ten camels unasked when [[Abraham]]'s servant arrives at her family's well — an act of extraordinary generosity. She leaves everything to marry a stranger. She hears the oracle: the elder will serve the younger. She makes it happen, using her husband's blindness. She never sees [[Jacob]] again after she saves him.

log.note: [[Rebekah]] hears that [[Isaac]] intends to bless [[Esau]]; she dresses [[Jacob]] in goat skins so blind [[Isaac]] will mistake him for his hairy brother, and sends [[Jacob]] to steal the blessing.

npcs.name: Jacob
npcs.role: Patriarch of Israel, heel-grabber and wrestler
npcs.description: He is born grabbing his twin's heel. He buys [[Esau]]'s birthright for stew. He steals [[Isaac]]'s blessing. He flees, dreams of a ladder, bargains with God. He works seven years for [[Rachel]] and is given [[Leah]] instead. He works seven more. He wrestles God at [[The Ford of Jabbok|the ford of Jabbok]] and refuses to let go. His name becomes Israel. He is the ancestor of everyone.

log.note: [[Jacob]] deceives his blind father and receives the blessing of the firstborn; [[Esau]] arrives minutes later and weeps: is he not rightly named [[Jacob]]? He has supplanted me twice. He plans to kill [[Jacob]] after [[Isaac]]'s death.

[[Jacob]] flees to [[Laban]]'s house and dreams of a ladder reaching to heaven with angels ascending and descending; God speaks from above and renews the covenant of [[Abraham]]. [[Jacob]] wakes: surely the LORD is in this place.
Consequence of Laban (ally): Laban substitutes Leah for Rachel — Jacob deceived as he deceived Isaac.

At [[The Ford of Jabbok]], [[Jacob]] wrestles with a man until daybreak; the man strikes [[Jacob]]'s hip but cannot pin him. [[Jacob]] refuses to release him without a blessing. The man asks his name. Then renames him: Israel.
Consequence of Joseph (ally): Joseph sold into Egypt by his own brothers.
Consequence of Joseph (ally): Joseph reveals himself — forgiveness, reunion, the dream fulfilled.

npcs.name: Esau
npcs.role: The elder twin, man of the field
npcs.description: He sells his birthright for a bowl of red stew because he is starving and cannot see past the moment. He weeps when he finds the blessing stolen — a sound that tears the reader in two. He forgives [[Jacob]] decades later and runs to meet him. His mercy is greater than his loss.

log.note: [[Esau]] comes in from the field famished and sells his birthright to [[Jacob]] for bread and a bowl of red lentil stew. He eats, rises, and goes away, having despised his birthright.
Consequence of Jacob (ally): Jacob steals Esau's blessing — Esau weeps, vows to kill him; Jacob must flee.

[[Esau]] runs to meet [[Jacob]] returning with his family and embraces him; [[Jacob]] had sent gifts ahead expecting violence and found forgiveness instead. [[Esau]] says: I have enough, my brother. Keep what you have.

npcs.name: Laban
npcs.role: Jacob's uncle and adversary
npcs.description: He substitutes [[Leah]] for [[Rachel]] on the wedding night and explains it away with local custom. He changes [[Jacob]]'s wages ten times over fourteen years. He pursues [[Jacob]] when he finally leaves and finds his household gods missing. He is the mirror [[Jacob]] needed: he meets a manipulator who out-manipulates him.

log.note: Consequence of Jacob (ally): Jacob steals Esau's blessing — Esau weeps, vows to kill him; Jacob must flee.

On the wedding night [[Laban]] substitutes [[Leah]] for [[Rachel]]; [[Jacob]] wakes to find the wrong wife. [[Laban]] explains: it is not done to give the younger before the firstborn. [[Jacob]] must work seven more years for [[Rachel]].

npcs.name: Rachel
npcs.role: Beloved wife of Jacob
npcs.description: [[Jacob]] sees her at the well and weeps. He works seven years for her. He works seven more. She steals her father's household gods when they leave — the text never explains why. She dies giving birth to Benjamin, naming him Son of My Sorrow with her last breath. [[Jacob]] renames him Son of My Right Hand.

log.note: Consequence of Jacob (ally): Jacob steals Esau's blessing — Esau weeps, vows to kill him; Jacob must flee.
Consequence of Joseph (ally): Joseph sold into Egypt by his own brothers.
Consequence of Joseph (ally): Joseph reveals himself — forgiveness, reunion, the dream fulfilled.

npcs.name: Leah
npcs.role: Elder wife of Jacob, unseen and fertile
npcs.description: She was substituted for her sister. The text says [[Jacob]] loved [[Rachel]] more. God sees Leah's unloved condition and opens her womb. She names her sons with prayers: Reuben — God has seen my affliction. Simeon — God heard I am unloved. Her naming of her children is a theology of pain acknowledged.

log.note: Consequence of Jacob (ally): Jacob steals Esau's blessing — Esau weeps, vows to kill him; Jacob must flee.

npcs.name: Joseph
npcs.role: Dreamer and Vizier of Egypt
npcs.description: [[Jacob]]'s favorite son, given a coat of many colors. His dreams say his brothers will bow to him. His brothers throw him in [[The Pit at Dothan|a pit]] and sell him to Ishmaelite traders for twenty pieces of silver. In Egypt he rises to the top of every system he enters — Potiphar's house, the prison, [[Pharaoh's Court|Pharaoh's court]]. He weeps when he finally sees his brothers again. He forgives them.

log.note: Consequence of Jacob (ally): Jacob steals Esau's blessing — Esau weeps, vows to kill him; Jacob must flee.
[[Joseph]] tells his dreams — sheaves bowing, stars bowing — to his father and brothers. His brothers hate him and cannot speak peaceably to him.
[[Joseph]] is stripped of his coat of many colors, thrown in a dry pit, and sold to Ishmaelite traders for twenty pieces of silver. He is carried down to Egypt.
In Egypt [[Joseph]] rises to oversee Potiphar's household; Potiphar's wife falsely accuses him of assault when he rejects her; he is thrown into prison.
In prison [[Joseph]] correctly interprets the dreams of [[Pharaoh]]'s cupbearer and baker; the cupbearer forgets him for two years.
[[Joseph]] recognizes his brothers but does not reveal himself; he tests them, accuses them of spying, keeps Simeon, and demands [[Benjamin]] be brought. He turns away and weeps.

[[Joseph]] can control himself no longer; he clears the room and weeps aloud. He says: I am [[Joseph]]. Is my father still alive? He cannot stop weeping. He kisses all his brothers and they talk together.

npcs.name: Judah
npcs.role: Fourth son of Jacob, the unexpected leader
npcs.description: He suggests selling [[Joseph]] instead of killing him. He fails his daughter-in-law Tamar. He stands surety for Benjamin before [[Jacob]]. In Egypt, when [[Pharaoh]]'s vizier threatens to keep Benjamin, it is Judah — not Reuben — who steps forward and offers himself as a substitute. That speech changes [[Joseph]]. The line of Judah becomes the royal line.

log.note: [[Judah]] persuades his brothers not to kill [[Joseph]] but to sell him to Ishmaelite traders for twenty pieces of silver. They dip his coat in goat's blood and bring it to [[Jacob]]: we found this. Is it your son's robe?
Consequence of Joseph (ally): Joseph sold into Egypt by his own brothers.

When [[Joseph]] threatens to keep [[Benjamin]], [[Judah]] steps forward and offers himself as a substitute: let me remain as your servant in place of the boy. How can I go back to my father if the boy is not with me?
Consequence of Joseph (ally): Joseph reveals himself — forgiveness, reunion, the dream fulfilled.

npcs.name: Pharaoh
npcs.role: King of Egypt, Joseph's patron
npcs.description: He dreams of seven fat cows and seven lean cows, and [[Joseph]] interprets it: seven years of plenty, then seven years of famine. He makes a Hebrew slave and prisoner second-in-command of all Egypt before breakfast. He is pragmatic, powerful, and — in Genesis — not yet an enemy.

log.note: Consequence of Joseph (ally): Joseph sold into Egypt by his own brothers.

[[Pharaoh]] dreams of seven fat cows and seven lean cows; [[Joseph]] interprets the dream: seven years of plenty, then seven years of famine. [[Pharaoh]] makes [[Joseph]] second in command of all Egypt.
Consequence of Joseph (ally): Joseph reveals himself — forgiveness, reunion, the dream fulfilled.
